content:
{"content": "     1|# Enki Browser 2 – AI‑First Browser Service
     2|
     3|## Overview
     4|Enki Browser 2 is an upgraded AI‑first browser control service built on **Node.js + Express + Playwright**.
     5|It provides a backend HTTP API for automated browser navigation, interaction, and artifact capture.
     6|
     7|Designed for integration with backend agents/automation systems, it enables reliable browser automation with persistent profiles, run‑level auditing, and optional open‑mode for unknown domains.
     8|
     9|## Installation
    10|
    11|```bash
    12|cd /home/openclaw/openclaw-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2
    13|npm install
    14|```
    15|
    16|### Playwright Browsers
    17|```bash
    18|npx playwright install chromium
    19|```
    20|
    21|## Configuration
    22|
    23|| Env var       | Default                                    | Description                                    |
    24||---------------|--------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
    25|| `PORT`        | `4318`                                     | HTTP port                                      |
    26|| `DATA_DIR`    | `./data`                                   | Data directory (profile, allowed domains, etc.)|
    27|| `CONFIG_DIR`  | `./config`                                 | Config directory (known‑good domains)          |
    28|| `RUNS_DIR`    | `./runs`                                   | Run artifacts folder                           |
    29|| `PROFILE_DIR` | `./data/profile`                           | Chromium persistent profile                    |
    30|| `ENKI_OPEN_MODE`| `true` (recommended)                      | When `true`, allow unknown domains             |
    31|
    32|If `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=false`, unknown domains are gated by approval workflow (approval‑gate is available in both modes).
    33|
    34|## Start the Service
    35|
    36|```bash
    37|cd /home/openclaw/openclaw-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2
    38|ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true PORT=4318 node server.js
    39|```
    40|
    41|PID is written to `.server.pid` when started via this command.
    42|
    43|## API Endpoints
    44|
    45|| Method | Endpoint                | Description                                                  |
    46||--------|-------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------|
    47|| GET    | `/health`               | Service health + configuration info                           |
    48|| GET    | `/policy`               | Current policy state (open mode, known good count, etc.)     |
    49|| POST   | `/navigate`             | Navigate to URL (body: `{"url": "https://..."}`)            |
    50|| POST   | `/open`                 | Alias for `/navigate`                                        |
    51|| POST   | `/screenshot`           | Returns screenshot PNG of active page                        |
    52|| POST   | `/screenshot/save`      | Save screenshot to run folder → `screenshot.png`             |
    53|| GET    | `/visible-text`         | Extract visible text and save `visible-text.txt` in run      |
    54|| GET    | `/dom-summary`          | Return DOM summary and save `dom-summary.json` in run        |
    55|| GET    | `/console`              | Return captured console messages (saved to `console.json`)   |
    56|| GET    | `/network-failures`     | Return failed network requests (saved to `network-failures.json`) |
    57|| POST   | `/click`                | Click element by selector (body: `{"selector": "a"}`)       |
    58|| POST   | `/fill`                 | Fill input field (body: `{"selector": "input", "value": "text"}`) |
    59|| POST   | `/press`                | Press a key (body: `{"key": "Enter"}`)                      |
    60|| POST   | `/run`                  | Execute a structured browser task and save run artifacts     |
    61|| GET    | `/runs`                 | List available run folders                                   |
    62|| GET    | `/runs/:id`             | Inspect run and list artifact files                          |
    63|
    64|## Run & Artifacts
    65|
    66|Each browser task creates a **timestamped run folder** under `runs/` (format `YYYY-MM-DDTHH-mm-ss-SSSZ`).
    67|Typical run artifacts:
    68|
    69|- `metadata.json`    — run metadata (runId, timestamps, URL, profile, openMode)
    70|- `screenshot.png`   — screenshot when requested
    71|- `visible-text.txt` — visible page text when requested
    72|- `dom-summary.json` — DOM summary when requested
    73|- `console.json`     — captured console messages
    74|- `network-failures.json` — captured failed network requests
    75|- `run.json`         — run description + action results
    76|
    77|## Policy / Approval Gate
    78|
    79|When `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=false`:
    80|- Unknown domains are blocked (403) until approved.
    81|- Approvals can be performed via the `/approvals/allow` endpoint (implemented in the approval layer; the service maintains allowed/pending lists under `data/`).
    82|
    83|When `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true`:
    84|- Unknown domains are allowed and logged in run metadata.
    85|- No navigation blocking occurs — but logs and policy stats remain available.
    86|
    87|## Examples
    88|
    89|### Health check
    90|```bash
    91|curl http://localhost:4318/health
    92|```
    93|
    94|### Navigate & capture
    95|```bash
    96|curl -X POST http://localhost:4318/navigate -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"url":"https://example.com"}'
    97|curl http://localhost:4318/visible-text
    98|curl http://localhost:4318/dom-summary
    99|curl http://localhost:4318/screenshot --output screenshot.png
   100|```
   101|
   102|### Execute structured run
   103|```bash
   104|curl -X POST http://localhost:4318/run -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{
   105|  "url": "https://example.com",
   106|  "actions": [
   107|    { "type": "screenshot" },
   108|    { "type": "visibleText" },
   109|    { "type": "domSummary" }
   110|  ]
   111|}'
   112|```
   113|
   114|
   115|## CLI Wrapper
   116|
   117|A convenience shell script `enki` is provided for quick CLI access to the API.
   118|
   119|```bash
   120|# Health check
   121|./enki health
   122|
   123|# Navigate to example.com
   124|./enki open https://example.com
   125|
   126|# Click a link (selector)
   127|./enki click "a"
   128|
   129|# Fill an input (selector + value)
   130|./enki fill "input[name=q]" "test value"
   131|
   132|# Press a key
   133|./enki press "Enter"
   134|
   135|# Get visible text
   136|./enki text
   137|
   138|# Get DOM summary
   139|./enki dom
   140|
   141|# Get console messages
   142|./enki console
   143|
   144|# Get network failures
   145|./enki network
   146|
   147|# Get page content (HTML)
   148|./enki content
   149|
   150|# Capture screenshot
   151|./enki screenshot
   152|
   153|# Inspect a specific run (provide runId)
   154|./enki inspect-run 2026-05-04T02-42-16.472Z
   155|
   156|# List run folders
   157|./enki runs
   158|
   159|# Run structured task (navigate + screenshot + text + dom)
   160|./enki run https://example.com
   161|```
   162|
   163|The wrapper defaults to `http://127.0.0.1:4318`; you can override the base URL with `ENKI_BROWSER_URL`:
   164|```bash
   165|ENKI_BROWSER_URL=http://yourhost:4318 ./enki health
   166|```
   167|
   168|## Smoke Test
   169|
   170|Run the smoke test sequence against `https://example.com` with `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true`:
   171|
   172|1. `GET /health`
   173|2. `POST /navigate` → https://example.com
   174|3. `GET /visible-text`
   175|4. `GET /dom-summary`
   176|5. `GET /console`
   177|6. `GET /network-failures`
   178|7. `POST /screenshot/save`
   179|
   180|A timestamped run folder with all expected files should be created in `runs/`.
   181|
   182|## Architecture Summary
   183|
   184|- **Node.js (CommonJS)** — runtime  
   185|- **Express** — HTTP API server  
   186|- **Playwright** — Chromium automation (persistent profile)  
   187|- **Chromium persistent context** — uses `data/profile`  
   188|- **Runs folder** — `runs/` stores per‑task artifacts  
   189|- **Open mode** – `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true` allows unknown domains (logs them instead of blocking)  
   190|- **Approval‑gate mode** – `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=false` retains old safety gate logic for unknown domains  
   191|
   192|## Known Limitations
   193|
   194|- Click/fill/press use selector‑only support (no text/role‑based fallback currently).  
   195|- Run folder is created lazily — some artifacts may not appear until after a triggering endpoint is called.  
   196|- Console and network logs accumulate in memory per active session and are written to disk when requested or at run completion.  
   197|- The service does not perform automatic browser updates or install browsers on start.  
   198|
   199|## Maintenance
   200|
   201|- Stop the service gracefully by killing the PID file’s process (`kill $(cat .server.pid)`).  
   202|- To clean runs, remove stale folders under `runs/`.  
   203|- To reset the browser profile, remove or rename `data/profile`.  
   204|
   205|## Security Notes
   206|
   207|- No remote shell/exec endpoints are exposed.  
   208|- Run logs may contain request/response metadata — redact secrets if sharing logs.  
   209|- The service is intended for controlled automation use within trusted environments.  
   210|", "total_lines": 209, "file_size": 8086, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

! I now understand the Enki Browser 2 structure. It's a Node.js + Express + Playwright service that provides an HTTP API for browser automation. Key details:

- Dependencies: Node.js, npm, Playwright (Chromium)
- Environment variables: PORT (default 4318), DATA_DIR, CONFIG_DIR, RUNS_DIR, PROFILE_DIR, ENKI_OPEN_MODE
- To start: `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true PORT=4318 node server.js`
- PID file: `.server.pid`

{"content": "     1|# Quick Commands for Enki Browser 2
     2|
     3|These are the simplest ways to interact with Enki Browser 2 from the command line using the wrapper script.
     4|
     5|## Basic Health & Policy
     6|```bash
     7|cd /home/openclaw/openclaw-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2
     8|./enki health      # Check if service is running
     9|./enki policy      # View open-mode and domain stats
    10|```
    11|
    12|## Navigation & Content
    13|```bash
    14|./enki open https://example.com   # Navigate to a URL
    15|./enki text                       # Extract visible text from current page
    16|./enki dom                        # Get DOM summary
    17|./enki content                     # Get full page HTML (POST)
    18|```
    19|
    20|## Screenshots & Runs
    21|```bash
    22|./enki screenshot                  # Capture and save screenshot to run folder
    23|./enki runs                       # List all run folders
    24|./enki inspect-run <runId>        # Inspect a specific run's artifacts
    25|./enki run https://example.com   # Structured run (navigate + screenshot + text + dom)
    26|```
    27|
    28|## Interactive Actions
    29|```bash
    30|./enki click "a"                  # Click an element by CSS selector
    31|./enki fill "input[name=q]" "value"  # Fill an input field
    32|./enki press "Enter"              # Press a key on the page
    33|```
    34|
    35|## Diagnostics
    36|```bash
    37|./enki console                    # Get captured console messages
    38|./enki network                    # Get failed network requests
    39|```
    40|
    41|## Overriding the Base URL
    42|If the service is running on a different host/port, override the default:
    43|```bash
    44|ENKI_BROWSER_URL=http://127.0.0.1:4318 ./enki health
    45|```
    46|
    47|## Typical Workflow
    48|```bash
    49|cd /home/openclaw/openclaw-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2
    50|./enki health                         # Ensure service is up
    51|./enki open https://example.com      # Navigate
    52|./enki screenshot                     # Capture screenshot
    53|./enki text                          # Get visible text
    54|./enki runs                          # Check run folders
    55|LATEST=$(./enki runs | python3 -c 'import sys,json; print(json.load(sys.stdin)["runs"][-1])')
    56|./enki inspect-run "$LATEST"          # Inspect latest run
    57|```

     3|**CLI Wrapper integration:** A lightweight `enki` script has been added to `/home/openclaw/openclaw-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2/enki` for convenient CLI access from the OC command‑center workflow.

     8|
     9|---
    10|

    36|
    37|## 4. Architecture Summary
    38|
    39|| Component | Implementation |
    40||-----------|----------------|

    48|
    49|## 5. Endpoint Status
    50|

    52||----------|-------------|-------|

    71|## 6. Logging Artifacts (Smoke Test Run)

! Now I'll create the `enki` CLI wrapper script. This will be a bash script that translates CLI commands into curl calls to the Enki Browser API.

I'll write it to be executable and include all the commands from the docs.

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"path": "/home/openclaw/hermes-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2/enki", "content": "#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Enki Browser CLI - Frontend for Enki Browser 2 API
# Usage: ./enki <command> [args...]

set -euo pipefail

# Default base URL (can override with ENKI_BROWSER_URL)
BASE_URL="${ENKI_BROWSER_URL:-http://127.0.0.1:4318}"

# Helper: print error and exit
err() { echo "ERROR: $*" >&2; exit 1; }

# Helper: curl JSON
curl_json() {
    local method=$1 url=$2 data=${3:-}
    if [[ -z "$data" ]]; then
        curl -sS -X "$method" "$BASE_URL$url" -H 'Content-Type: application/json'
    else
        curl -sS -X "$method" "$BASE_URL$url" -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d "$data"
    fi
}

# Commands
case "${1:-}" in
    help|--help|-h)
        cat <<'EOF'
Enki Browser CLI - Commands:
  health                 GET  /health
  policy                 GET  /policy
  open <url>             POST /navigate (also: navigate)
  text                   GET  /visible-text
  dom                    GET  /dom-summary
  content                GET  /content
  console                GET  /console
  network                GET  /network-failures
  screenshot             POST /screenshot (outputs PNG to stdout)
  screenshot/save        POST /screenshot/save
  click <selector>       POST /click {"selector":"..."}
  fill <selector> <val>  POST /fill {"selector":"...","value":"..."}
  press <key>            POST /press {"key":"..."}
  run <url>              POST /run {"url":"...","actions":[{"type":"screenshot"},{"type":"visibleText"},{"type":"domSummary"}]}
  runs                   GET  /runs
  inspect-run <runId>    GET  /runs/:id
EOF
        exit 0
        ;;
    health)
        curl_json GET /health
        ;;
    policy)
        curl_json GET /policy
        ;;
    open|navigate)
        [[ -n "${2:-}" ]] || err "Usage: $0 open <url>"
        curl_json POST /navigate "{\"url\":\"$2\"}"
        ;;
    text)
        curl_json GET /visible-text
        ;;
    dom)
        curl_json GET /dom-summary
        ;;
    content)
        curl_json GET /content
        ;;
    console)
        curl_json GET /console
        ;;
    network)
        curl_json GET /network-failures
        ;;
    screenshot)
        curl -sS -X POST "$BASE_URL/screenshot" -H 'Content-Type: application/json' --output - || err "Failed to get screenshot"
        ;;
    screenshot/save)
        curl -sS -X POST "$BASE_URL/screenshot/save" -H 'Content-Type: application/json' || err "Failed to save screenshot"
        ;;
    click)
        [[ -n "${2:-}" ]] || err "Usage: $0 click <selector>"
        curl_json POST /click "{\"selector\":\"$2\"}"
        ;;
    fill)
        [[ -n "${2:-}" && -n "${3:-}" ]] || err "Usage: $0 fill <selector> <value>"
        curl_json POST /fill "{\"selector\":\"$2\",\"value\":\"$3\"}"
        ;;
    press)
        [[ -n "${2:-}" ]] || err "Usage: $0 press <key>"
        curl_json POST /press "{\"key\":\"$2\"}"
        ;;
    run)
        [[ -n "${2:-}" ]] || err "Usage: $0 run <url>"
        # Structured run: navigate + screenshot + visibleText + domSummary
        curl_json POST /run "{\"url\":\"$2\",\"actions\":[{\"type\":\"screenshot\"},{\"type\":\"visibleText\"},{\"type\":\"domSummary\"}]}"
        ;;
    runs)
        curl_json GET /runs
        ;;
    inspect-run)
        [[ -n "${2:-}" ]] || err "Usage: $0 inspect-run <runId>"
        curl_json GET "/runs/$2"
        ;;
    "")
        err "No command given. Try './enki help' for usage."
        ;;
    *)
        err "Unknown command: $1. Try './enki help'."
        ;;
esac
"}

```
---
name: enki-browser
description: Enki Browser 2 – AI-first browser service
category: productivity
tags: [browser, automation, playwright, http-api]
---

# Enki Browser Skill

Enki Browser 2 is a dedicated browser automation service for agentic AI. It runs as a background service providing a simple HTTP API for navigation, interaction, and artifact capture.

- Service: http://127.0.0.1:4318
- CLI wrapper: `enki` (available system-wide after adding to PATH)
- Workspace data: `data/profile` (persistent Chromium profile), `runs/` (artifacts), `config/known_good_domains.json`
- Open mode: enabled by default, allowing unknown domains.

## Usage

### From the command line (CLI)

```bash
# Health check
enki health

# Navigate to a URL
enki open https://example.com

# Get page text
enki text

# Get DOM summary
enki dom

# Capture screenshot (prints PNG to stdout)
enki screenshot > screenshot.png

# Structured run: navigate + screenshot + text + dom
enki run https://example.com

# Run a series of actions (see quick commands)
# Full list: enki help
```

### From within Hermes (via terminal tool)

You can invoke the CLI directly:

```bash
terminal: enki open https://example.com
terminal: enki text
terminal: enki screenshot --output screenshot.png
```

Or use `curl` against the API:

```bash
terminal: curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:4318/navigate -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"url":"https://example.com"}'
terminal: curl http://127.0.0.1:4318/visible-text
```

## Common Tasks

- **Open a page and capture text + screenshot**:
  `enki run https://example.com`
  This creates a timestamped run folder under `runs/`.

- **Click an element**:
  `enki click "button.some-class"`

- **Fill a form**:
  `enki fill "input[name='email']" "test@example.com"`

- **Press a key**:
  `enki press "Enter"`

## Pitfalls

- Selectors only: click/fill/press rely on CSS selectors. No text-based fallback.
- Run artifacts are written lazily; after a run, some files may appear later.
- Console and network logs accumulate in memory; they are saved when requested or at run completion.
- The service runs under your user account; ensure port 4318 is open in your firewall if accessing remotely.
- If you change the port, update the CLI's `ENKI_BROWSER_URL` or the direct calls.

## References

- Enki Browser 2 README: `~/hermes-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2/README.md`
- API spec: `/health`, `/policy`, `/navigate`, `/open`, `/screenshot`, `/visible-text`, `/dom-summary`, `/content`, `/console`, `/network-failures`, `/click`, `/fill`, `/press`, `/run`, `/runs`, `/runs/:id`
```

---
name: enki-browser
description: Enki Browser 2 – AI-first browser service
category: productivity
tags: [browser, automation, playwright, http-api]
---

# Enki Browser Skill

Enki Browser 2 is a dedicated browser automation service for agentic AI. It runs as a background service providing a simple HTTP API for navigation, interaction, and artifact capture.

- Service: http://127.0.0.1:4318
- CLI wrapper: `enki` (available system-wide after adding to PATH)
- Workspace data: `data/profile` (persistent Chromium profile), `runs/` (artifacts), `config/known_good_domains.json`
- Open mode: enabled by default, allowing unknown domains.

## Usage

### From the command line (CLI)

```bash
# Health check
enki health

# Navigate to a URL
enki open https://example.com

# Get page text
enki text

# Get DOM summary
enki dom

# Capture screenshot (prints PNG to stdout)
enki screenshot > screenshot.png

# Structured run: navigate + screenshot + text + dom
enki run https://example.com

# Run a series of actions (see quick commands)
# Full list: enki help
```

### From within Hermes (via terminal tool)

You can invoke the CLI directly:

```bash
terminal: enki open https://example.com
terminal: enki text
terminal: enki screenshot --output screenshot.png
```

Or use `curl` against the API:

```bash
terminal: curl -X POST http://127.0.0.1:4318/navigate -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"url":"https://example.com"}'
terminal: curl http://127.0.0.1:4318/visible-text
```

## Common Tasks

- **Open a page and capture text + screenshot**:
  `enki run https://example.com`
  This creates a timestamped run folder under `runs/`.

- **Click an element**:
  `enki click "button.some-class"`

- **Fill a form**:
  `enki fill "input[name='email']" "test@example.com"`

- **Press a key**:
  `enki press "Enter"`

## Pitfalls

- Selectors only: click/fill/press rely on CSS selectors. No text-based fallback.
- Run artifacts are written lazily; after a run, some files may appear later.
- Console and network logs accumulate in memory; they are saved when requested or at run completion.
- The service runs under your user account; ensure port 4318 is open in your firewall if accessing remotely.
- If you change the port, update the CLI's `ENKI_BROWSER_URL` or the direct calls.

## References

- Enki Browser 2 README: `~/hermes-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2/README.md`
- API spec: `/health`, `/policy`, `/navigate`, `/open`, `/screenshot`, `/visible-text`, `/dom-summary`, `/content`, `/console`, `/network-failures`, `/click`, `/fill`, `/press`, `/run`, `/runs`, `/runs/:id`
```

This skill provides tools for controlling the Enki Browser 2 service, an AI-first browser designed for agentic automation.

# Enki Browser Skill

This skill provides tools for controlling the Enki Browser 2 service, an AI-first browser designed for agentic automation.

## Prerequisites

The Enki Browser service must be running (systemd user service `enki-browser.service`). It listens on `127.0.0.1:4318`.

## Tools

- `enki_health`: Returns service status and config.
- `enki_navigate(url)`: Navigates to a URL.
- `enki_visible_text()`: Extracts visible text from the current page.
- `enki_dom_summary()`: Returns a structured summary of the DOM.
- `enki_click(selector)`: Clicks an element matching the CSS selector.
- `enki_fill(selector, value)`: Fills an input field.
- `enki_press(key)`: Simulates a key press.
- `enki_run_structured(url)`: Runs a full capture sequence on a URL (screenshot, text, dom).

## Examples

```
enki_navigate("https://example.com")
enki_visible_text()
enki_click("a.link")
```

## Note

The CLI wrapper is located at `/home/openclaw/hermes-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2/enki`. The skill uses absolute paths to ensure it can be invoked from any context.
```

# Enki Browser Skill

This skill provides tools for controlling the Enki Browser 2 service, an AI-first browser designed for agentic automation.

## Prerequisites

The Enki Browser service must be running (systemd user service `enki-browser.service`). It listens on `127.0.0.1:4318`.

## Tools

- `enki_health()`: Returns service status and config.
- `enki_navigate(url)`: Navigates to a URL.
- `enki_visible_text()`: Extracts visible text from the current page.
- `enki_dom_summary()`: Returns a structured summary of the DOM.
- `enki_click(selector)`: Clicks an element matching the CSS selector.
- `enki_fill(selector, value)`: Fills an input field.
- `enki_press(key)`: Simulates a key press.
- `enki_run_structured(url)`: Runs a full capture sequence on a URL (screenshot, text, dom).

## Examples

```
enki_navigate("https://example.com")
enki_visible_text()
enki_click("a.link")
```

---
name: enki-browser
description: Interact with the Enki Browser 2 service (AI-first browser) via the enki CLI. Enables navigation, clicking, filling forms, screenshots, and extracting page content.
---
# Enki Browser Tool

Enki Browser 2 is a headless Chromium automation service with a persistent profile. It is designed for agentic workflows.

## Service
The service runs as a systemd user unit: `enki-browser.service`. It starts automatically on login. To enable at boot: `sudo loginctl enable-linger openclaw`.

The service listens on `127.0.0.1:4318` with `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true`.

## CLI Wrapper
The CLI wrapper script is installed at `/home/openclaw/hermes-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2/enki`. It can be invoked directly or via the symlink `enki` if added to PATH.

## Common Commands
- `enki health` – check service status
- `enki open <url>` – navigate to a URL
- `enki text` – extract visible text
- `enki dom` – get DOM summary
- `enki screenshot` – capture PNG (outputs to stdout)
- `enki screenshot/save` – save to run folder
- `enki click "<selector>"` – click element
- `enki fill "<selector>" "<value>"` – fill input
- `enki press "<key>"` – press key
- `enki run <url>` – execute a structured run (navigate+artifacts)
- `enki runs` – list run folders
- `enki inspect-run <runId>` – inspect artifacts

## Integration
Hermes can call this tool by executing the CLI via the terminal tool. For example:

```bash
./enki open https://example.com
./enki text
```

Each run creates a timestamped folder under `runs/` with artifacts (screenshot, visible-text.txt, dom-summary.json, etc.).

## Notes
- The service uses a persistent Chromium profile at `data/profile`.
- In open mode, unknown domains are allowed and logged.
```

---
name: enki-browser

# Enki Browser Tool

Enki Browser 2 is a headless Chromium automation service with a persistent profile, designed for agentic workflows. It runs as a systemd user service (`enki-browser.service`) and is controlled via the `enki` CLI wrapper.

## Prerequisites
- The service must be running. (It starts on login; for boot auto-start, run `sudo loginctl enable-linger openclaw`.)
- The `enki` CLI is available at `/home/openclaw/hermes-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2/enki`. A symlink is installed to `~/.local/bin/enki`.

## CLI Commands
Key commands (use `enki help` for full list):
- `enki health` – check service health
- `enki open <url>` – navigate to URL
- `enki screenshot` – capture PNG to stdout
- `enki screenshot/save` – save to current run folder
- `enki text` – extract visible page text
- `enki dom` – get DOM summary
- `enki click "<selector>"` – click element by CSS selector
- `enki fill "<selector>" "<value>"` – fill input field
- `enki press "<key>"` – press a key
- `enki run <url>` – structured run (navigate + screenshot + text + dom)
- `enki runs` – list run folders
- `enki inspect-run <runId>` – inspect artifacts

## Usage from Hermes
Hermes can invoke these commands via the terminal tool, e.g.:
```bash
enki open https://example.com
enki text > page.txt
```
The service creates timestamped run artifacts under `runs/` (screenshots, text, JSON).

## Notes
- Service port: 4318 (127.0.0.1:4318)
- `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true` by default, allowing any domain (gate optional).
- Persistent profile at `data/profile` retains cookies/localStorage across runs.

---
name: enki-browser
description: Interact with the Enki Browser 2 service (AI-first browser) via the enki CLI. Enables navigation, clicking, filling forms, screenshots, and extracting page content.
---
# Enki Browser Tool

Enki Browser 2 is a headless Chromium automation service with a persistent profile. It is designed for agentic workflows.

## Service
The service runs as a systemd user unit: `enki-browser.service`. It starts automatically on login. To enable at boot: `sudo loginctl enable-linger openclaw`.

The service listens on `127.0.0.1:4318` with `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true`.

## CLI Wrapper
The CLI wrapper script is installed at `/home/openclaw/hermes-command-center/tools/enki-browser-2/enki`. It can be invoked directly or via the symlink `enki` if added to PATH.

## Common Commands
- `enki health` – check service status
- `enki open <url>` – navigate to a URL
- `enki text` – extract visible text
- `enki dom` – get DOM summary
- `enki screenshot` – capture PNG (outputs to stdout)
- `enki screenshot/save` – save to run folder
- `enki click "<selector>"` – click element
- `enki fill "<selector>" "<value>"` – fill input
- `enki press "<key>"` – press key
- `enki run <url>` – execute a structured run (navigate+artifacts)
- `enki runs` – list run folders
- `enki inspect-run <runId>` – inspect artifacts

## Integration
Hermes can call this tool by executing the CLI via the terminal tool. For example:

```bash
./enki open https://example.com
./enki text
```

Each run creates a timestamped folder under `runs/` with artifacts (screenshot, visible-text.txt, dom-summary.json, etc.).

## Notes
- The service uses a persistent Chromium profile at `data/profile`.
- In open mode, unknown domains are allowed and logged.
```

# Enki Browser Tool

Enki Browser 2 is a headless Chromium automation service with a persistent profile, designed for agentic workflows. It runs as a systemd user service (`enki-browser.service`) and is controlled via the `enki` CLI wrapper.

- `enki dom` – get DOM summary

## Notes

- `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true` by default, allowing any domain (gate optional).
- Persistent profile at `data/profile` retains cookies/localStorage across runs.

# Enki Browser Tool

Enki Browser 2 is a headless Chromium automation service with a persistent profile, designed for agentic workflows. It runs as a systemd user service (`enki-browser.service`) and is controlled via the `enki` CLI wrapper.

## Prerequisites
- The service must be running. (It starts on login; for boot auto-start, run `sudo loginctl enable-linger openclaw`.)

- `enki screenshot` – capture PNG to stdout
- `enki screenshot/save` – save to current run folder
- `enki text` – extract visible page text
- `enki dom` – get DOM summary
- `enki click "<selector>"` – click element by CSS selector
- `enki fill "<selector>" "<value>"` – fill input field
- `enki press "<key>"` – press a key
- `enki run <url>` – structured run (navigate + screenshot + text + dom)
- `enki runs` – list run folders
- `enki inspect-run <runId>` – inspect artifacts

## Notes
- Service port: 4318 (127.0.0.1:4318)
- `ENKI_OPEN_MODE=true` by default, allowing any domain (gate optional).
- Persistent profile at `data/profile` retains cookies/localStorage across runs.

{"code": "import requests, json, math
from datetime import datetime

# Fetch daily OHLC for Bitcoin (vs USD) from CoinGecko
url = "https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3/coins/bitcoin/ohlc"
params = {"vs_currency": "usd", "days": "100"}  # last 100 days
resp = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=10)
resp.raise_for_status()
data = resp.json()  # format: [[timestamp, open, high, low, close], ...]

closes = [d[4] for d in data]
highs = [d[2] for d in data]
lows = [d[3] for d in data]

# Compute indicators
def sma(period):
    sma_vals = []
    for i in range(len(closes)):
        if i < period-1:
            sma_vals.append(None)
        else:
            sma_vals.append(sum(closes[i-period+1:i+1]) / period)
    return sma_vals

def rsi(period=14):
    rsi_vals = []
    gains = []
    losses = []
    for i in range(1, len(closes)):
        change = closes[i] - closes[i-1]
        gains.append(change if change > 0 else 0)
        losses.append(-change if change < 0 else 0)
    for i in range(period, len(gains)+1):
        avg_gain = sum(gains[i-period:i]) / period
        avg_loss = sum(losses[i-period:i]) / period
        if avg_loss == 0:
            rs = float('inf')
        else:
            rs = avg_gain / avg_loss
        rsi_val = 100 - (100 / (1 + rs))
        rsi_vals.append(rsi_val)
    return [None]*period + rsi_vals

sma20 = sma(20)
sma50 = sma(50)
rsi14 = rsi(14)

latest_close = closes[-1]
latest_sma20 = sma20[-1]
latest_sma50 = sma50[-1]
latest_rsi = rsi14[-1]

recent_high = max(highs[-20:])
recent_low = min(lows[-20:])

# volatility
volatility = sum([abs(closes[i] - closes[i-1])/closes[i-1] for i in range(1, len(closes))]) / (len(closes)-1) * 100

print("BTC/USDT latest close:", latest_close)
print("SMA20:", latest_sma20)
print("SMA50:", latest_sma50)
print("RSI(14):", latest_rsi)
print("20-day High:", recent_high)
print("20-day Low:", recent_low)
print("Avg daily volatility (last 100d):", f"{volatility:.2f}%")

# Trend determination
if latest_close > latest_sma20 and latest_sma20 > latest_sma50:
    trend = "Uptrend (price > SMA20 > SMA50)"
elif latest_close < latest_sma20 and latest_sma20 < latest_sma50:
    trend = "Downtrend (price < SMA20 < SMA50)"
else:
    trend = "Consolidation/Neutral"
print("Trend:", trend)

# Support/Resistance
support = recent_low
resistance = recent_high
print("Key support (20d low):", f"${support:,.2f}")
print("Key resistance (20d high):", f"${resistance:,.2f}")

# 2-week statistical range
daily_vol = latest_close * (volatility/100)
two_week_vol = daily_vol * math.sqrt(14)
lower_2sd = latest_close - 2 * two_week_vol
upper_2sd = latest_close + 2 * two_week_vol
print(f"Statistical 2-week range (95% confidence): ${lower_2sd:,.2f} - ${upper_2sd:,.2f}")

# Print RSI condition
if latest_rsi > 70:
    rsi_state = "Overbought"
elif latest_rsi < 30:
    rsi_state = "Oversold"
else:
    rsi_state = "Neutral"
print("RSI state:", rsi_state)"}

{"code": "import requests, json, math
from datetime import datetime

# Fetch 30 days of daily price data from CoinGecko

data = resp.json()

# data['prices'] is list of [timestamp, price] for each day (approx 00:00 UTC)
prices = [p[1] for p in data["prices"]]
# The last element is the current (or near current) price
closes = prices

print("Fetched", len(closes), "days of closing prices")
print("Most recent closes:", closes[-5:])

# Compute SMA20, SMA50 requires at least 50 days; we only have 30. So we can compute SMA20 (with 20 days) and maybe not SMA50.
def sma(period):
    if len(closes) < period:
        return None, None
    sma_val = sum(closes[-period:]) / period
    return sma_val

sma20 = sma(20)
# We don't have 50 days, so compute from available up to maybe 30 if we want SMA30.
sma30 = sma(30) if len(closes) >= 30 else None

# Compute RSI(14)
def compute_rsi(prices, period=14):
    if len(prices) <= period:
        return None
    gains = []
    losses = []
    for i in range(1, len(prices)):
        change = prices[i] - prices[i-1]
        gains.append(change if change > 0 else 0)
        losses.append(-change if change < 0 else 0)
    # Use last 'period' gains/losses
    last_gains = gains[-period:]
    last_losses = losses[-period:]
    avg_gain = sum(last_gains) / period
    avg_loss = sum(last_losses) / period
    if avg_loss == 0:
        rs = float('inf')
    else:
        rs = avg_gain / avg_loss
    rsi = 100 - (100 / (1 + rs))
    return rsi

rsi14 = compute_rsi(closes, 14)

avg_return = sum(returns)/len(returns)
# Use standard deviation as volatility

print(f"SMA30: {sma30:,.2f}" if sma30 else "SMA30: insufficient data")

print(f"Approx 20-day low (min close): {recent_low_approx:,.2f}")

# Trend based on price vs sma20 and price momentum

{"code": "import requests, json, math
from datetime import datetime

# Fetch 30 days of daily price data from CoinGecko
url = "https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3/coins/bitcoin/market_chart"
params = {"vs_currency": "usd", "days": "30", "interval": "daily"}
resp = requests.get(url, params=params, timeout=10)
resp.raise_for_status()
data = resp.json()

# Extract closing prices (the 'prices' array: [timestamp, price])
closes = [p[1] for p in data["prices"]]
print(f"Fetched {len(closes)} days of closing prices. Latest: ${closes[-1]:,.2f}")

# Compute SMA20
sma20 = sum(closes[-20:]) / 20 if len(closes) >= 20 else None
print(f"SMA20: ${sma20:,.2f}" if sma20 else "SMA20: insufficient data (<20 days)")

# Compute RSI(14)
def rsi(prices, period=14):
    if len(prices) <= period:
        return None
    gains = []
    losses = []
    for i in range(1, len(prices)):
        change = prices[i] - prices[i-1]
        gains.append(change if change > 0 else 0)
        losses.append(-change if change < 0 else 0)
    last_gains = gains[-period:]
    last_losses = losses[-period:]
    avg_gain = sum(last_gains) / period
    avg_loss = sum(last_losses) / period
    if avg_loss == 0:
        rs = float('inf')
    else:
        rs = avg_gain / avg_loss
    return 100 - (100 / (1 + rs))
rsi14 = rsi(closes, 14)
print(f"RSI(14): {rsi14:.2f}" if rsi14 else "RSI: insufficient data")

# Approximate support/resistance using recent close extremes (20 days)
recent_closes = closes[-20:] if len(closes) >= 20 else closes
approx_support = min(recent_closes)
approx_resistance = max(recent_closes)
print(f"Approx support (20d low of closes): ${approx_support:,.2f}")
print(f"Approx resistance (20d high of closes): ${approx_resistance:,.2f}")

# Daily volatility (stdev of returns)
returns = [(closes[i] - closes[i-1])/closes[i-1] for i in range(1, len(closes))]
avg_ret = sum(returns)/len(returns)
vol = math.sqrt(sum((r - avg_ret)**2 for r in returns) / len(returns)) * 100
print(f"Daily volatility (stdev): {vol:.2f}%")

# Trend: compare latest close to sma20
if sma20 and closes[-1] > sma20:
    trend = "Above 20-day SMA suggests short-term bullish"
else:
    trend = "Below 20-day SMA suggests short-term bearish"
print("Trend bias:", trend)

# 2-week (14 day) statistical range using volatility
current = closes[-1]
daily_vol_amt = current * (vol/100)
two_week_vol = daily_vol_amt * math.sqrt(14)
lower_2sd = current - 2 * two_week_vol
upper_2sd = current + 2 * two_week_vol
print(f"95% confidence 2-week range: ${lower_2sd:,.2f} - ${upper_2sd:,.2f}")"}

version: 1.3.0
author: gamedevCloudy
license: MIT
metadata:
  hermes:
    tags: [search, duckduckgo, web-search, free, fallback]
    related_skills: [arxiv]
    fallback_for_toolsets: [web]
---

# DuckDuckGo Search

Free web search using DuckDuckGo. **No API key required.**

Preferred when `web_search` is unavailable or unsuitable (for example when `FIRECRAWL_API_KEY` is not set). Can also be used as a standalone search path when DuckDuckGo results are specifically desired.

## Detection Flow

Check what is actually available before choosing an approach:

```bash
# Check CLI availability
command -v ddgs >/dev/null && echo "DDGS_CLI=installed" || echo "DDGS_CLI=missing"
```

Decision tree:
1. If `ddgs` CLI is installed, prefer `terminal` + `ddgs`
2. If `ddgs` CLI is missing, do not assume `execute_code` can import `ddgs`
3. If the user wants DuckDuckGo specifically, install `ddgs` first in the relevant environment
4. Otherwise fall back to built-in web/browser tools

Important runtime note:
- Terminal and `execute_code` are separate runtimes
- A successful shell install does not guarantee `execute_code` can import `ddgs`
- Never assume third-party Python packages are preinstalled inside `execute_code`

## Installation

Install `ddgs` only when DuckDuckGo search is specifically needed and the runtime does not already provide it.

```bash
# Python package + CLI entrypoint
pip install ddgs

# Verify CLI
ddgs --help
```

If a workflow depends on Python imports, verify that same runtime can import `ddgs` before using `from ddgs import DDGS`.

## Method 1: CLI Search (Preferred)

Use the `ddgs` command via `terminal` when it exists. This is the preferred path because it avoids assuming the `execute_code` sandbox has the `ddgs` Python package installed.

```bash
# Text search
ddgs text -q "python async programming" -m 5

# News search
ddgs news -q "artificial intelligence" -m 5

# Image search
ddgs images -q "landscape photography" -m 10

# Video search
ddgs videos -q "python tutorial" -m 5

# With region filter
ddgs text -q "best restaurants" -m 5 -r us-en

# Recent results only (d=day, w=week, m=month, y=year)
ddgs text -q "latest AI news" -m 5 -t w

# JSON output for parsing
ddgs text -q "fastapi tutorial" -m 5 -o json
```

### CLI Flags

| Flag | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| `-q` | Query — **required** | `-q "search terms"` |
| `-m` | Max results | `-m 5` |
| `-r` | Region | `-r us-en` |
| `-t` | Time limit | `-t w` (week) |
| `-s` | Safe search | `-s off` |
| `-o` | Output format | `-o json` |

## Method 2: Python API (Only After Verification)

Use the `DDGS` class in `execute_code` or another Python runtime only after verifying that `ddgs` is installed there. Do not assume `execute_code` includes third-party packages by default.

Safe wording:
- "Use `execute_code` with `ddgs` after installing or verifying the package if needed"

Avoid saying:
- "`execute_code` includes `ddgs`"
- "DuckDuckGo search works by default in `execute_code`"

**Important:** `max_results` must always be passed as a **keyword argument** — positional usage raises an error on all methods.

### Text Search

Best for: general research, companies, documentation.

```python
from ddgs import DDGS

with DDGS() as ddgs:
    for r in ddgs.text("python async programming", max_results=5):
        print(r["title"])
        print(r["href"])
        print(r.get("body", "")[:200])
        print()
```

Returns: `title`, `href`, `body`

### News Search

Best for: current events, breaking news, latest updates.

```python
from ddgs import DDGS

with DDGS() as ddgs:
    for r in ddgs.news("AI regulation 2026", max_results=5):
        print(r["date"], "-", r["title"])
        print(r.get("source", ""), "|", r["url"])
        print(r.get("body", "")[:200])
        print()
```

Returns: `date`, `title`, `body`, `url`, `image`, `source`

### Image Search

Best for: visual references, product images, diagrams.

```python
from ddgs import DDGS

with DDGS() as ddgs:
    for r in ddgs.images("semiconductor chip", max_results=5):
        print(r["title"])
        print(r["image"])
        print(r.get("thumbnail", ""))
        print(r.get("source", ""))
        print()
```

Returns: `title`, `image`, `thumbnail`, `url`, `height`, `width`, `source`

### Video Search

Best for: tutorials, demos, explainers.

```python
from ddgs import DDGS

with DDGS() as ddgs:
    for r in ddgs.videos("FastAPI tutorial", max_results=5):
        print(r["title"])
        print(r.get("content", ""))
        print(r.get("duration", ""))
        print(r.get("provider", ""))
        print(r.get("published", ""))
        print()
```

Returns: `title`, `content`, `description`, `duration`, `provider`, `published`, `statistics`, `uploader`

### Quick Reference

| Method | Use When | Key Fields |
|--------|----------|------------|
| `text()` | General research, companies | title, href, body |
| `news()` | Current events, updates | date, title, source, body, url |
| `images()` | Visuals, diagrams | title, image, thumbnail, url |
| `videos()` | Tutorials, demos | title, content, duration, provider |

## Workflow: Search then Extract

DuckDuckGo returns titles, URLs, and snippets — not full page content. To get full page content, search first and then extract the most relevant URL with `web_extract`, browser tools, or curl.

CLI example:

```bash
ddgs text -q "fastapi deployment guide" -m 3 -o json
```

Python example, only after verifying `ddgs` is installed in that runtime:

```python
from ddgs import DDGS

with DDGS() as ddgs:
    results = list(ddgs.text("fastapi deployment guide", max_results=3))
    for r in results:
        print(r["title"], "->", r["href"])
```

Then extract the best URL with `web_extract` or another content-retrieval tool.

## Limitations

- **Rate limiting**: DuckDuckGo may throttle after many rapid requests. Add a short delay between searches if needed.
- **No content extraction**: `ddgs` returns snippets, not full page content. Use `web_extract`, browser tools, or curl for the full article/page.
- **Results quality**: Generally good but less configurable than Firecrawl's search.
- **Availability**: DuckDuckGo may block requests from some cloud IPs. If searches return empty, try different keywords or wait a few seconds.
- **Field variability**: Return fields may vary between results or `ddgs` versions. Use `.get()` for optional fields to avoid `KeyError`.
- **Separate runtimes**: A successful `ddgs` install in terminal does not automatically mean `execute_code` can import it.

## Troubleshooting

| Problem | Likely Cause | What To Do |
|---------|--------------|------------|
| `ddgs: command not found` | CLI not installed in the shell environment | Install `ddgs`, or use built-in web/browser tools instead |
| `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'ddgs'` | Python runtime does not have the package installed | Do not use Python DDGS there until that runtime is prepared |
| Search returns nothing | Temporary rate limiting or poor query | Wait a few seconds, retry, or adjust the query |
| CLI works but `execute_code` import fails | Terminal and `execute_code` are different runtimes | Keep using CLI, or separately prepare the Python runtime |

## Pitfalls

- **`max_results` is keyword-only**: `ddgs.text("query", 5)` raises an error. Use `ddgs.text("query", max_results=5)`.
- **Do not assume the CLI exists**: Check `command -v ddgs` before using it.
- **Do not assume `execute_code` can import `ddgs`**: `from ddgs import DDGS` may fail with `ModuleNotFoundError` unless that runtime was prepared separately.
- **Package name**: The package is `ddgs` (previously `duckduckgo-search`). Install with `pip install ddgs`.
- **Don't confuse `-q` and `-m`** (CLI): `-q` is for the query, `-m` is for max results count.
- **Empty results**: If `ddgs` returns nothing, it may be rate-limited. Wait a few seconds and retry.

## Validated With

Validated examples against `ddgs==9.11.2` semantics. Skill guidance now treats CLI availability and Python import availability as separate concerns so the documented workflow matches actual runtime behavior.

function.name: execute_code
function.arguments:
{"code": "import urllib.request, xml.etree.ElementTree as ET, datetime, re

url = "https://news.google.com/rss/search?q=bitcoin&hl=en-US&gl=US&ceid=US:en"
# Fetch the RSS feed
headers = {'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0 (compatible; Hermes/1.0)'}
req = urllib.request.Request(url, headers=headers)
data = urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=10).read()

# Parse XML
root = ET.fromstring(data)
# Google News RSS namespace: typically none, but items are under channel/item
channel = root.find('channel')
items = channel.findall('item') if channel is not None else root.findall('.//item')

# Extract title, link, pubDate, description
articles = []
for item in items[:10]:  # top 10 recent
    title = item.find('title').text
    link = item.find('link').text
    pubDate_str = item.find('pubDate').text
    # Convert pubDate to datetime (RFC 2822)
    try:
        pub_date = datetime.datetime.strptime(pubDate_str, '%a, %d %b %Y %H:%M:%S %Z')
    except:
        pub_date = None
    description = item.find('description').text if item.find('description') is not None else ''
    # Clean description: often contains HTML; strip tags
    if description:
        description = re.sub('<[^<]+?>', '', description).strip()
    articles.append({
        'title': title,
        'link': link,
        'pub_date': pub_date_str,
        'description': description
    })

print(f"Fetched {len(articles)} articles from Google News Bitcoin RSS")
for i, a in enumerate(articles, 1):
    print(f"{i}. {a['title']} ({a['pub_date']})")
    print(f"   {a['description'][:200]}..." if len(a['description']) > 200 else f"   {a['description']}")
    print(f"   Link: {a['link']}\n")

# Determine recency: we can filter to last 7 days maybe.
# But for analysis, we just need key themes. We'll later summarize."}

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import json, os, sys
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

JOURNAL_DIR = os.path.expanduser('~/hermes-command-center/crypto-journal')
PREDICTIONS_FILE = os.path.join(JOURNAL_DIR, 'predictions.jsonl')
ROTATION_FILE = os.path.join(JOURNAL_DIR, 'rotation_state.json')

TOOLS = ['integrated-technical-news', 'pure-technical', 'momentum']

def load_rotation():
    if os.path.exists(ROTATION_FILE):
        with open(ROTATION_FILE) as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            return data.get('next_index', 0)
    return 0

def save_rotation(next_idx):
    with open(ROTATION_FILE, 'w') as f:
        json.dump({'next_index': next_idx}, f)

def log_prediction():
    # read JSON from stdin
    data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    # Add id if not present
    if 'id' not in data:
        # generate: pred-YYYYMMDD-XXXXX
        date_str = data.get('date', datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d'))
        # use timestamp + random?
        unique = datetime.now().strftime('%H%M%S')
        data['id'] = f"pred-{date_str.replace('-', '')}-{unique}"
    # Ensure outcome null initially
    data.setdefault('outcome', None)
    data.setdefault('actual_price', None)
    data.setdefault('pnl_percent', None)
    # Append to file
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'a') as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(data) + '\n')
    print(json.dumps({"status":"logged","id":data['id']}))

def record_outcome():
    data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    # data must include id or date+symbol? We'll use id.
    if 'id' not in data:
        raise ValueError("Missing 'id' in outcome payload")
    # Read all predictions, update matching
    updated = False
    lines = []
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'r') as f:
        for line in f:
            p = json.loads(line)
            if p['id'] == data['id']:
                p['actual_price'] = data['actual_price']
                p['outcome'] = data.get('outcome')
                p['pnl_percent'] = data.get('pnl_percent')
                updated = True
            lines.append(p)
    if not updated:
        raise ValueError(f"Prediction ID {data['id']} not found")
    # Write back
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'w') as f:
        for p in lines:
            f.write(json.dumps(p) + '\n')
    print(json.dumps({"status":"updated","id":data['id']}))

def evaluate_due(auto=False):
    # auto: automatically fetch price and record outcomes
    if auto:
        # get current BTC price via CoinGecko
        import requests
        try:
            resp = requests.get('https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3/simple/price', params={'ids':'bitcoin','vs_currencies':'usd'}, timeout=10)
            resp.raise_for_status()
            current_price = resp.json()['bitcoin']['usd']
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Error fetching price: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
            return
    # Read predictions
    due = []
    lines = []
    today = datetime.now().date()
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'r') as f:
        for line in f:
            p = json.loads(line)
            # Check if due and not yet evaluated
            if p.get('outcome') is None:
                pred_date = datetime.strptime(p['date'], '%Y-%m-%d').date()
                horizon = p['horizon_days']
                if today >= pred_date + timedelta(days=horizon):
                    due.append(p)
            lines.append(p)

    if not due:
        print("No due predictions to evaluate.")
        return

    updates = []
    for p in due:
        if auto:
            entry = p['entry_price']
            actual = current_price
            # Determine outcome
            direction = p['predicted_direction']
            if direction == 'bullish':
                outcome = 'win' if actual > entry else 'loss'
            elif direction == 'bearish':
                outcome = 'win' if actual < entry else 'loss'
            else:
                outcome = 'neutral'
            pnl = ((actual - entry) / entry) * 100
            p['actual_price'] = actual
            p['outcome'] = outcome
            p['pnl_percent'] = pnl
            updates.append(p)
        else:
            # Just list due predictions
            print(f"Due: {p['id']} {p['symbol']} {p['date']} +{p['horizon_days']}d entry={p['entry_price']}")

    if auto and updates:
        # Write back updated file
        with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'w') as f:
            for p in lines:
                # if p is in updates, use updated version
                for u in updates:
                    if p['id'] == u['id']:
                        p = u
                        break
                f.write(json.dumps(p) + '\n')
        print(f"Automatically updated {len(updates)} predictions with current price ${current_price}")
    elif auto:
        print("No updates made.")

def stats():
    # Compute overall stats and by tool
    if not os.path.exists(PREDICTIONS_FILE):
        print("No predictions found.")
        return
    data = []
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'r') as f:
        for line in f:
            p = json.loads(line)
            data.append(p)
    total = len(data)
    evaluated = [p for p in data if p.get('outcome') is not None]
    win_count = sum(1 for p in evaluated if p['outcome'] == 'win')
    loss_count = len(evaluated) - win_count
    avg_pnl = sum(p['pnl_percent'] for p in evaluated) / len(evaluated) if evaluated else 0
    # By tool
    by_tool = {}
    for p in evaluated:
        tool = p.get('tool','unknown')
        if tool not in by_tool:
            by_tool[tool] = {'count':0, 'wins':0, 'pnl_sum':0}
        by_tool[tool]['count'] += 1
        if p['outcome'] == 'win':
            by_tool[tool]['wins'] += 1
        by_tool[tool]['pnl_sum'] += p['pnl_percent']
    # Print summary
    print(f"Total predictions: {total}")
    print(f"Evaluated: {len(evaluated)}")
    if evaluated:
        print(f"Overall win rate: {win_count}/{len(evaluated)} = {win_count/len(evaluated)*100:.1f}%")
        print(f"Average PnL: {avg_pnl:.2f}%")
    print("\nBy tool:")
    for tool, stats in by_tool.items():
        wr = stats['wins']/stats['count']*100 if stats['count'] else 0
        avg = stats['pnl_sum']/stats['count']
        print(f"  {tool}: {stats['wins']}/{stats['count']} ({wr:.1f}% win), avg pnl {avg:.2f}%")

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import json, os, sys, requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
import math
import re

# Configuration
JOURNAL_DIR = os.path.expanduser('~/hermes-command-center/crypto-journal')
PREDICTIONS_FILE = os.path.join(JOURNAL_DIR, 'predictions.jsonl')
ROTATION_FILE = os.path.join(JOURNAL_DIR, 'rotation_state.json')
TOOLS = ['integrated-technical-news', 'pure-technical', 'momentum']

# Ensure journal dir exists
os.makedirs(JOURNAL_DIR, exist_ok=True)

# ---------- Helper functions ----------
def load_rotation():
    if os.path.exists(ROTATION_FILE):
        try:
            with open(ROTATION_FILE) as f:
                data = json.load(f)
                return data.get('next_index', 0)
        except:
            return 0
    return 0

def save_rotation(idx):
    with open(ROTATION_FILE, 'w') as f:
        json.dump({'next_index': idx}, f)

def fetch_json(url, params=None):
    headers = {'User-Agent': 'HermesCrypto/1.0'}
    resp = requests.get(url, params=params, headers=headers, timeout=10)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    return resp.json()

def fetch_price_history(days=30):
    # CoinGecko market_chart
    url = "https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3/coins/bitcoin/market_chart"
    params = {"vs_currency": "usd", "days": str(days), "interval": "daily"}
    data = fetch_json(url, params=params)
    closes = [p[1] for p in data["prices"]]
    return closes

def compute_sma(closes, period):
    if len(closes) < period:
        return None
    return sum(closes[-period:]) / period

def compute_rsi(closes, period=14):
    if len(closes) <= period:
        return None
    gains = []
    losses = []
    for i in range(1, len(closes)):
        change = closes[i] - closes[i-1]
        gains.append(change if change > 0 else 0)
        losses.append(-change if change < 0 else 0)
    last_gains = gains[-period:]
    last_losses = losses[-period:]
    avg_gain = sum(last_gains) / period
    avg_loss = sum(last_losses) / period
    if avg_loss == 0:
        rs = float('inf')
    else:
        rs = avg_gain / avg_loss
    return 100 - (100 / (1 + rs))

def fetch_news_sentiment():
    # Fetch Google News RSS for bitcoin
    url = "https://news.google.com/rss/search?q=bitcoin&hl=en-US&gl=US&ceid=US:en"
    headers = {'User-Agent': 'HermesCrypto/1.0'}
    resp = requests.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=10)
    resp.raise_for_status()
    # Parse XML (use built-in)
    import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET
    root = ET.fromstring(resp.content)
    channel = root.find('channel')
    items = channel.findall('item') if channel is not None else root.findall('.//item')
    bullish_words = ['bull', 'bullish', 'rise', 'surge', 'gain', 'up', 'positive', 'strong', 'strength', 'soar', 'rocket', 'higher', 'outperform', 'beat', 'rebound', 'recover']
    bearish_words = ['bear', 'bearish', 'fall', 'drop', 'crash', 'down', 'negative', 'weak', 'loss', 'decline', 'plunge', 'lower', 'underperform', 'miss', 'slump', 'dip']
    net_score = 0
    for item in items[:10]:
        title = item.find('title').text or ''
        desc = item.find('description').text or ''
        text = (title + ' ' + desc).lower()
        for w in bullish_words:
            net_score += len(re.findall(r'\b' + re.escape(w) + r'\b', text))
        for w in bearish_words:
            net_score -= len(re.findall(r'\b' + re.escape(w) + r'\b', text))
    return net_score

def get_current_price():
    data = fetch_json("https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3/simple/price", params={"ids":"bitcoin","vs_currencies":"usd"})
    return data["bitcoin"]["usd"]

# ---------- Prediction logic per tool ----------
def predict_integrated():
    # Fetch data
    closes = fetch_price_history(days=30)
    current_price = closes[-1]
    sma20 = compute_sma(closes, 20)
    rsi = compute_rsi(closes, 14)
    # Technical signal
    if sma20 is None or rsi is None:
        direction = 'bullish'  # default
        base_conf = 0.5
    else:
        # Determine direction
        if rsi > 70 and current_price > sma20:
            direction = 'bearish'
        elif rsi < 30 and current_price < sma20:
            direction = 'bullish'
        elif current_price > sma20:
            direction = 'bullish'
        else:
            direction = 'bearish'
        # Base confidence based on RSI extremity
        base_conf = 0.6
        if rsi > 70 or rsi < 30:
            base_conf += 0.1
    # Sentiment check
    try:
        sentiment = fetch_news_sentiment()
    except Exception as e:
        sentiment = 0
    sentiment_adj = 0
    if sentiment > 0:
        sentiment_adj = 0.1 if direction == 'bullish' else -0.1
    elif sentiment < 0:
        sentiment_adj = -0.1 if direction == 'bullish' else 0.1
    confidence = max(0.1, min(0.95, base_conf + sentiment_adj))
    notes = f"Technical: price {'above' if current_price > sma20 else 'below'} SMA20, RSI={rsi:.1f}. News sentiment score={sentiment}."
    return direction, confidence, notes

def predict_pure_technical():
    closes = fetch_price_history(days=30)
    current_price = closes[-1]
    sma20 = compute_sma(closes, 20)
    if sma20 is None:
        direction = 'bullish'
        confidence = 0.5
        notes = "Insufficient data for SMA, default bullish."
    else:
        direction = 'bullish' if current_price > sma20 else 'bearish'
        confidence = 0.55  # modest
        notes = f"Price {'above' if direction=='bullish' else 'below'} SMA20 only."
    return direction, confidence, notes

def predict_momentum():
    closes = fetch_price_history(days=30)
    if len(closes) < 8:
        direction = 'bullish'
        confidence = 0.5
        notes = "Insufficient data for 7‑day momentum."
    else:
        week_return = (closes[-1] - closes[-8]) / closes[-8]
        direction = 'bullish' if week_return > 0 else 'bearish'
        confidence = min(0.7, 0.5 + abs(week_return)*10)
        notes = f"7‑day return {week_return:.2%}."
    return direction, confidence, notes

# ---------- Command implementations ----------
def log_prediction():
    data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    if 'id' not in data:
        date_str = data.get('date', datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')).replace('-','')
        unique = datetime.now().strftime('%H%M%S')
        data['id'] = f"pred-{date_str}-{unique}"
    data.setdefault('outcome', None)
    data.setdefault('actual_price', None)
    data.setdefault('pnl_percent', None)
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'a') as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(data) + '\n')
    print(json.dumps({"status":"logged","id":data['id']}))

def record_outcome():
    data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    if 'id' not in data:
        raise ValueError("Missing 'id'")
    updated = False
    lines = []
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'r') as f:
        for line in f:
            p = json.loads(line)
            if p['id'] == data['id']:
                p['actual_price'] = data['actual_price']
                p['outcome'] = data.get('outcome')
                p['pnl_percent'] = data.get('pnl_percent')
                updated = True
            lines.append(p)
    if not updated:
        raise ValueError(f"ID {data['id']} not found")
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'w') as f:
        for p in lines:
            f.write(json.dumps(p) + '\n')
    print(json.dumps({"status":"updated","id":data['id']}))

def evaluate_due(auto=False):
    today = datetime.now().date()
    due = []
    lines = []
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'r') as f:
        for line in f:
            p = json.loads(line)
            if p.get('outcome') is None:
                pred_date = datetime.strptime(p['date'], '%Y-%m-%d').date()
                horizon = p['horizon_days']
                if today >= pred_date + timedelta(days=horizon):
                    due.append(p)
            lines.append(p)
    if not due:
        print("No due predictions.")
        return
    if auto:
        # Fetch current price once
        try:
            current_price = get_current_price()
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Failed to fetch price: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
            return
        updates = []
        for p in due:
            entry = p['entry_price']
            actual = current_price
            direction = p['predicted_direction']
            if direction == 'bullish':
                outcome = 'win' if actual > entry else 'loss'
            elif direction == 'bearish':
                outcome = 'win' if actual < entry else 'loss'
            else:
                outcome = 'neutral'
            pnl = ((actual - entry) / entry) * 100
            p['actual_price'] = actual
            p['outcome'] = outcome
            p['pnl_percent'] = pnl
            updates.append(p)
        # Write back
        with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'w') as f:
            for p in lines:
                for u in updates:
                    if p['id'] == u['id']:
                        p = u
                        break
                f.write(json.dumps(p) + '\n')
        print(f"Updated {len(updates)} predictions with price ${current_price:,.2f}.")
    else:
        for p in due:
            print(f"Due: {p['id']} {p['symbol']} entry={p['entry_price']} predicted={p['predicted_direction']}")

def stats():
    if not os.path.exists(PREDICTIONS_FILE):
        print("No predictions.")
        return
    data = []
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'r') as f:
        for line in f:
            data.append(json.loads(line))
    total = len(data)
    evaluated = [p for p in data if p.get('outcome') is not None]
    win_count = sum(1 for p in evaluated if p['outcome'] == 'win')
    loss_count = len(evaluated) - win_count
    avg_pnl = sum(p.get('pnl_percent',0) for p in evaluated) / len(evaluated) if evaluated else 0
    by_tool = {}
    for p in evaluated:
        tool = p.get('tool','unknown')
        by_tool.setdefault(tool, {'count':0, 'wins':0, 'pnl_sum':0})
        by_tool[tool]['count'] += 1
        if p['outcome'] == 'win':
            by_tool[tool]['wins'] += 1
        by_tool[tool]['pnl_sum'] += p.get('pnl_percent',0)
    print(f"Total: {total}, Evaluated: {len(evaluated)}")
    if evaluated:
        print(f"Overall win rate: {win_count}/{len(evaluated)} ({win_count/len(evaluated)*100:.1f}%)")
        print(f"Average PnL: {avg_pnl:.2f}%")
    print("\nBy tool:")
    for tool, st in by_tool.items():
        wr = st['wins']/st['count']*100 if st['count'] else 0
        avg = st['pnl_sum']/st['count']
        print(f"  {tool}: {st['wins']}/{st['count']} ({wr:.1f}%), avg pnl {avg:.2f}%")

def predict():
    idx = load_rotation()
    tool = TOOLS[idx]
    next_idx = (idx + 1) % len(TOOLS)
    save_rotation(next_idx)
    # Generate
    try:
        current_price = get_current_price()
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error fetching price: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    if tool == 'integrated-technical-news':
        direction, confidence, notes = predict_integrated()
    elif tool == 'pure-technical':
        direction, confidence, notes = predict_pure_technical()
    elif tool == 'momentum':
        direction, confidence, notes = predict_momentum()
    else:
        import random
        direction = random.choice(['bullish','bearish'])
        confidence = 0.5
        notes = "Random fallback."
    # Build prediction
    date_str = datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
    unique = datetime.now().strftime('%H%M%S')
    pred = {
        "id": f"pred-{date_str.replace('-','')}-{unique}",
        "date": date_str,
        "symbol": "BTC/USDT",
        "horizon_days": 14,
        "tool": tool,
        "entry_price": current_price,
        "predicted_direction": direction,
        "confidence": confidence,
        "notes": notes,
        "outcome": None,
        "actual_price": None,
        "pnl_percent": None
    }
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'a') as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(pred) + '\n')
    # Output summary to stdout for cron/log
    print(json.dumps({
        "status": "predicted",
        "id": pred["id"],
        "tool": tool,
        "direction": direction,
        "confidence": confidence,
        "entry_price": current_price
    }))

def main():
    if len(sys.argv) < 2:
        print("Usage: crypto_journal.py {log_prediction|record_outcome|evaluate_due|stats|predict}")
        sys.exit(1)
    cmd = sys.argv[1]
    if cmd == 'log_prediction':
        log_prediction()
    elif cmd == 'record_outcome':
        record_outcome()
    elif cmd == 'evaluate_due':
        auto = '--auto' in sys.argv
        evaluate_due(auto=auto)
    elif cmd == 'stats':
        stats()
    elif cmd == 'predict':
        predict()
    else:
        print(f"Unknown command: {cmd}")
        sys.exit(1)

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

#!/usr/bin/env python3
import json, os, sys
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

JOURNAL_DIR = os.path.expanduser('~/hermes-command-center/crypto-journal')
PREDICTIONS_FILE = os.path.join(JOURNAL_DIR, 'predictions.jsonl')
ROTATION_FILE = os.path.join(JOURNAL_DIR, 'rotation_state.json')

TOOLS = ['integrated-technical-news', 'pure-technical', 'momentum']

def load_rotation():
    if os.path.exists(ROTATION_FILE):
        with open(ROTATION_FILE) as f:
            data = json.load(f)
            return data.get('next_index', 0)
    return 0

def save_rotation(next_idx):
    with open(ROTATION_FILE, 'w') as f:
        json.dump({'next_index': next_idx}, f)

def log_prediction():
    # read JSON from stdin
    data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    # Add id if not present
    if 'id' not in data:
        # generate: pred-YYYYMMDD-XXXXX
        date_str = data.get('date', datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d'))
        # use timestamp + random?
        unique = datetime.now().strftime('%H%M%S')
        data['id'] = f"pred-{date_str.replace('-', '')}-{unique}"
    # Ensure outcome null initially
    data.setdefault('outcome', None)
    data.setdefault('actual_price', None)
    data.setdefault('pnl_percent', None)
    # Append to file
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'a') as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(data) + '\n')
    print(json.dumps({"status":"logged","id":data['id']}))

def record_outcome():
    data = json.load(sys.stdin)
    # data must include id or date+symbol? We'll use id.
    if 'id' not in data:
        raise ValueError("Missing 'id' in outcome payload")
    # Read all predictions, update matching
    updated = False
    lines = []
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'r') as f:
        for line in f:
            p = json.loads(line)
            if p['id'] == data['id']:
                p['actual_price'] = data['actual_price']
                p['outcome'] = data.get('outcome')
                p['pnl_percent'] = data.get('pnl_percent')
                updated = True
            lines.append(p)
    if not updated:
        raise ValueError(f"Prediction ID {data['id']} not found")
    # Write back
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'w') as f:
        for p in lines:
            f.write(json.dumps(p) + '\n')
    print(json.dumps({"status":"updated","id":data['id']}))

def evaluate_due(auto=False):
    # auto: automatically fetch price and record outcomes
    if auto:
        # get current BTC price via CoinGecko
        import requests
        try:
            resp = requests.get('https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3/simple/price', params={'ids':'bitcoin','vs_currencies':'usd'}, timeout=10)
            resp.raise_for_status()
            current_price = resp.json()['bitcoin']['usd']
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Error fetching price: {e}", file=sys.stderr)
            return
    # Read predictions
    due = []
    lines = []
    today = datetime.now().date()
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'r') as f:
        for line in f:
            p = json.loads(line)
            # Check if due and not yet evaluated
            if p.get('outcome') is None:
                pred_date = datetime.strptime(p['date'], '%Y-%m-%d').date()
                horizon = p['horizon_days']
                if today >= pred_date + timedelta(days=horizon):
                    due.append(p)
            lines.append(p)

    if not due:
        print("No due predictions to evaluate.")
        return

    updates = []
    for p in due:
        if auto:
            entry = p['entry_price']
            actual = current_price
            # Determine outcome
            direction = p['predicted_direction']
            if direction == 'bullish':
                outcome = 'win' if actual > entry else 'loss'
            elif direction == 'bearish':
                outcome = 'win' if actual < entry else 'loss'
            else:
                outcome = 'neutral'
            pnl = ((actual - entry) / entry) * 100
            p['actual_price'] = actual
            p['outcome'] = outcome
            p['pnl_percent'] = pnl
            updates.append(p)
        else:
            # Just list due predictions
            print(f"Due: {p['id']} {p['symbol']} {p['date']} +{p['horizon_days']}d entry={p['entry_price']}")

    if auto and updates:
        # Write back updated file
        with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'w') as f:
            for p in lines:
                # if p is in updates, use updated version
                for u in updates:
                    if p['id'] == u['id']:
                        p = u
                        break
                f.write(json.dumps(p) + '\n')
        print(f"Automatically updated {len(updates)} predictions with current price ${current_price}")
    elif auto:
        print("No updates made.")

def stats():
    # Compute overall stats and by tool
    if not os.path.exists(PREDICTIONS_FILE):
        print("No predictions found.")
        return
    data = []
    with open(PREDICTIONS_FILE, 'r') as f:
        for line in f:
            p = json.loads(line)
            data.append(p)
    total = len(data)
    evaluated = [p for p in data if p.get('outcome') is not None]
    win_count = sum(1 for p in evaluated if p['outcome'] == 'win')
    loss_count = len(evaluated) - win_count
    avg_pnl = sum(p['pnl_percent'] for p in evaluated) / len(evaluated) if evaluated else 0
    # By tool
    by_tool = {}
    for p in evaluated:
        tool = p.get('tool','unknown')
        if tool not in by_tool:
            by_tool[tool] = {'count':0, 'wins':0, 'pnl_sum':0}
        by_tool[tool]['count'] += 1
        if p['outcome'] == 'win':
            by_tool[tool]['wins'] += 1
        by_tool[tool]['pnl_sum'] += p['pnl_percent']
    # Print summary
    print(f"Total predictions: {total}")
    print(f"Evaluated: {len(evaluated)}")
    if evaluated:
        print(f"Overall win rate: {win_count}/{len(evaluated)} = {win_count/len(evaluated)*100:.1f}%")
        print(f"Average PnL: {avg_pnl:.2f}%")
    print("\nBy tool:")
    for tool, stats in by_tool.items():
        wr = stats['wins']/stats['count']*100 if stats['count'] else 0
        avg = stats['pnl_sum']/stats['count']
        print(f"  {tool}: {stats['wins']}/{stats['count']} ({wr:.1f}% win), avg pnl {avg:.2f}%")
